text: Root of inferior mesenteric artery bleeding during robotic D, three lymph node dissection for rectal cancer, a video vignette. This is a seventy-one-year-old man who presented with hematochesia for three months. Coloscopy showed a mass in upper rectum. Biopsy revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. No distant metastasis.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart on the operating table, which is set in a Trendelenburg position with a 30-degree angle and a right lateral inclination of 25 degrees. The surgical setup includes eight 8mm trocars and one 12mm assistant trocar.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon from the left abdominal wall, followed by mobilization of the sigmoid mesentery using a medial approach.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin. An attempt to dissect the skeletonized artery using a permanent cautery hook results in vascular avulsion at the root of the artery.

The surgeon uses finastrated bipolar forceps to apply compression hemostasis at the bleeding site. The permanent cautery hook is replaced with a needle driver, and a figure-eight suture combined with a running suture is performed using a 4-0 Prolene suture to control the bleeding.
The surgeon reinforces the bleeding site with another 4-0 Prolene suture, ensuring hemostasis as no active bleeding is visible.

Then, TME and end-to-end colorectal anastomosis were performed conventionally. The operation time was three hours and twenty minutes. The estimated blood loss was one hundred milliliters. The patient was discharged on postoperative day seven.

There are some tips for the procedure. Tip one, permanent cautery hook should not be used for the transection of major vessels because of the potential thermal conduction. Tip two, in the situation of IMA root injury, use the compression to control the bleeding immediately. Tip three, robotic repair of major vascular injuries with proline suturing could be attempted by experienced surgeons.
In conclusion, bleeding at root of IMA may be managed safely in robotic setting, thereby avoiding conversion to laparotomy. Thank you for your attention.
